ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code refers to boiled potato broadly; peeled specification not separately coded, hence 'closest_parent' match type.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium peeled boiled potato weighing approximately 150 g, served plain as a side dish or component of a meal.

nutrient_source.title: Boiled potato, peeled — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F14070006 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11356, Potatoes, boiled, cooked without skin, flesh, without salt) used to supplement saturated_fat_g (0.03 g/100g) and additional micronutrients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 82 (±3) for white potato, boiled, peeled, from University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (82 × 18.15) / 100 ≈ 14.9 for a 150 g serving (18.15 g available carbohydrate). Input record cited GI 85 from an embedded GI database with confidence 0.429; replaced with University of Sydney GI Database value of 82 per source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (8 mg/100g) and high potassium (316 mg/100g) supporting vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Boiled peeled potato is suitable for hypertension management due to its very low sodium content and high potassium level, which supports blood pressure reduction per DASH diet principles; avoid adding salt, butter, or high-sodium sauces post-cooking.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 82) with moderate carbohydrate (12.1 g/100g) and low fibre (2.0 g/100g) causing rapid postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled peeled potato has a high GI of 82 and should be consumed in small portions by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; cooling after cooking increases resistant starch and lowers effective GI, and pairing with protein and non-starchy vegetables further moderates the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.03 g/100g), zero cholesterol, and high potassium supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled peeled potato is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, making it a heart-friendly carbohydrate source when consumed plain and in appropriate portions; its potassium content supports arterial function, but portion control is advised to manage overall carbohydrate load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetable containing solanine, which may trigger joint inflammation in susceptible individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Potato is a nightshade vegetable; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor for joint flares after consumption, as solanine may exacerbate inflammation in sensitive patients, and no strong anti-inflammatory nutrients are present to offset this risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled potato, peeled (HPB Singapore cr_id F14070006) is a white-fleshed starchy vegetable prepared by boiling a peeled potato in water until soft and tender. The skin is removed before or after boiling, which reduces resistant starch content and raises the glycemic index compared to skin-on preparations. The texture is soft and slightly fluffy, with a mild, earthy, subtly sweet flavour. It is a globally consumed staple side dish and is common across Singaporean, Western, South Asian, and Southeast Asian cuisines. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 61.7 kcal, 1.5 g protein, 0.1 g fat, 12.1 g carbohydrate, 2.0 g dietary fibre, and 8 mg sodium. It is notably high in potassium (316 mg/100g), making it beneficial for blood pressure management under the DASH diet framework. However, its high glycemic index (GI 82, University of Sydney GI Database) makes it a concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; cooling after cooking increases resistant starch and lowers the effective GI. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher. It is a nightshade vegetable and may be unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to solanine. Suitable as a side dish, component of stews, or base for mashed preparations.
common_user_queries: Is boiled potato good for diabetics?
How many calories in a boiled potato?
Is boiled potato high in potassium?
Can I eat boiled potato if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of boiled potato?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14070006, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat and selected micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11356), a primary approved source. GI value replaced from University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008) per source policy, superseding the lower-confidence embedded GI value in the input.
notes: Energy value in input was 258.0 kJ (kilojoules); converted to 61.7 kcal (÷ 4.184) for the per_100g energy_kcal field to align with RESULT template units. Saturated_fat_g (0.03), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.002), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.043), trans_fat_g (0.0), and selected micronutrients (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 11356 (Potatoes, boiled, cooked without skin, flesh, without salt) as HPB did not report these fields. vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: not reported in HPB or USDA SR Legacy for this preparation. GI value updated from input value of 85 (confidence 0.429, embedded GI database) to 82 per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated for 150 g serving: (82 × 18.15 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 14.9. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (1.5), fat_g (0.1), and carbohydrate_g (12.1) are all non-zero. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source — potato is a plant food containing no cholesterol. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — plant food contains no vitamin B12. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for boiled peeled potato. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — no trans fats in plain boiled potato. Multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as this is a globally consumed food commonly encountered in Singaporean dietary contexts. Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese local_names set to null as this food is not cuisine-specific to those traditions and no mandatory multilingual requirement applies beyond the Singaporean context covered.
